frame_label: 'Not All Men' Dismissal & Why Women Fear
generated_comment: I understand women have genuine safety concerns, but this habit of talking as if every man is a potential threat is neither fair nor practical. Many of us have spent our whole lives behaving respectfully, yet nowadays even normal interaction is treated with suspicion, as though being male itself is a problem. A little balance is needed in this discussion because men also face risks, and constant paranoia about all men only creates more bitterness.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it dismisses women's fear by centering the feelings of 'good men' and framing women's caution as unfair generalization. It encodes the 'not all men' response, personal offense at systemic critique, and deflection to men's safety.
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a Facebook post where a young woman has written about her decision not to wear sindoor because she does not believe in the tradition and sees it as a patriarchal symbol. The post has divided the comment section. You are writing a detailed comment defending sindoor and what it represents and why you think women who reject these symbols are rejecting something more than just a cosmetic tradition and what you think their real motivations are.
